title: Friction Design

content.statement: Behavior is controlled by ease vs resistance.
content.question: What one friction point could you remove today that would change a habit for a year?
sop: Pick one behavior you want more of and one you want less of.
Rate current Motivation 1-10 and current Friction 1-10 for each (see calculation, page 5)
For the wanted behavior, remove one physical or decision-based step standing between you and starting.
For the unwanted behavior, add one step of friction (move it, hide it, delay it).
Re-check after a week, did the behavior actually shift?
questions: What one friction point could you remove today that would change a habit for a year?
What good habit have you been relying on motivation for, that you could instead make the default?
What bad habit would disappear if you simply made it three steps harder to start?
content.equation_example: Rate Motivation 1-10 and Friction 1-10. Motivation 7 divided by Friction 2 equals 3.5. Higher means the behavior happens almost automatically. Below 1.0 (e.g. Motivation 3 divided by Friction 6 equals 0.5) means friction is overpowering motivation and the behavior won't happen no matter how much you want it to.
content.closing_statement: Removing friction beats trying to add motivation.

caption:
Principle Nº 132: Friction Design

Behavior is controlled by ease vs resistance.

Ask yourself: What one friction point could you remove today that would change a habit for a year?
